title: How DraftKings and PrizePicks Are Legal in Texas | Daily Fantasy Law Explained
subtitle: Texas has not legalized online sports betting, yet millions of Texans play DraftKings and PrizePicks legally every week. Here is exactly why.
thumbnail_alt: Texas state capitol building with sports betting app on phone in foreground
content_html:
Texas is one of the most restrictive states in the country on traditional sports betting. As of May 2026, the Texas legislature has not passed a bill to legalize online sportsbooks. There is no DraftKings Sportsbook app for Texas residents. There is no FanDuel futures betting. And yet, millions of Texans open DraftKings and PrizePicks every Sunday during football season and play for real money. Legally.

The reason is a single legal distinction that the daily fantasy industry has successfully defended for more than a decade: daily fantasy sports are classified as a game of skill, not a game of chance, and games of skill are not gambling under Texas law.

The Texas Penal Code and the Skill-Game Carve-Out

Texas Penal Code Section 47.01 defines gambling as a bet on the partial or final result of a game or contest, or the performance of a participant in a game or contest. The critical qualifier is that a bet is a monetary agreement that depends on chance. Under Texas law, an activity where the outcome depends primarily on skill, not chance, falls outside the definition of illegal gambling.

Daily fantasy sports operators, including DraftKings and PrizePicks, built their legal argument on the premise that selecting players, analyzing matchups, assessing injury reports, and weighing statistical projections constitutes skill. A knowledgeable fantasy player with access to the right data will consistently outperform a random picker over a large sample size. That consistency is the hallmark of a skill-based activity.

The same argument is enshrined at the federal level. The Unlawful Internet Gambling Enforcement Act of 2006 explicitly carves out fantasy sports from the definition of illegal internet gambling, provided that the outcome reflects relative knowledge and skill of participants, the outcome is not based on the score or performance of a single athlete, and the prizes are established before the contest begins.

How DraftKings Qualifies | Salary-Cap Lineup Construction

DraftKings DFS requires users to build a team under a salary cap. Each player is assigned a dollar value based on expected production, and users must select a full lineup: quarterback, running backs, wide receivers, flex, tight end, defense, for a total salary at or under $50,000. The skill is in identifying undervalued players, projecting game script, exploiting correlations, and building lineups differentiated from the field in large-contest formats.

Texas has never moved to challenge DraftKings' DFS operations, and the company has operated in the state continuously since 2014. DraftKings holds a DFS license in Texas and pays applicable taxes. It does not, however, offer sportsbook wagering, live betting, or futures markets to Texas users, all of which require a sports betting license the state does not issue.

How PrizePicks Qualifies | The Pick'em Projection Model

PrizePicks operates on a similar legal foundation but with a simpler product. Users select between two and five players and predict whether each will go over or under a statistical projection, for example, Patrick Mahomes throwing more or fewer than 265.5 passing yards. If all selections are correct, the payout multiplies the entry by 3x for two picks, 5x for three picks, and up to 20x for five picks.

PrizePicks has faced legal scrutiny in states like New York and Nevada, which concluded that the Pick'em format more closely resembles traditional sports betting than multi-player fantasy competition. Texas has not taken that position. The state attorney general has not issued an opinion targeting Pick'em products, and PrizePicks is openly available to Texas users with full regulatory transparency, posting its Texas DFS license on the app.

What Texas Sports Betting Legalization Would Change

If Texas legalizes online sports betting, likely through a constitutional amendment requiring a voter referendum, DraftKings and PrizePicks would both apply for sportsbook licenses. The market impact would be significant. Texas has more than 30 million residents and three NFL teams in Dallas, Houston, and San Antonio. Analysts estimate a legal Texas sportsbook market could generate $1 to $2 billion in annual gross gaming revenue.

Until that happens, both companies continue operating in Texas under DFS licenses, earning revenue on one of the largest sports audiences in the country without the state capturing sportsbook tax revenue. It is a legal gap that benefits operators and Texans who want the product, and costs the state treasury nothing in the current framework because DFS is taxed as a skill-game business rather than a gambling operation.

Reported by Max DeLeonardis, OzoneNews Finance and Texas Desk.
